tldr: Both are transactional email APIs over REST + SMTP, so the wire-level switch is mostly drop-in. Real work is re-verifying DKIM/SPF on each sending domain, porting templates (Resend leans on React Email), and rebuilding webhooks and suppression lists. Mailgun-specific features like inbound Routes and email validation have no 1:1 Resend equivalent and may need third-party replacements.
why_switch: Modern, developer-first API with concise SDKs (Node, Python, Ruby, Go, PHP, Rust)
First-class React Email integration — author templates as React components instead of Handlebars
React Email is open source, lowering template lock-in
Cleaner dashboard and logs UX vs. Mailgun's older console
Simpler hybrid pricing with a 3,000 emails/mo free tier (per Resend pricing page)
SMTP relay still available, so existing SMTP-based code paths keep working
Active product velocity and migration guides published from common competitors
what_to_export: Verified sending domains and their DKIM, SPF, DMARC, MX records
Email templates and stored versions (Mailgun template store)
Mailing list / contacts data if used for transactional sends
Inbound Routes and their match expressions / forward actions
Webhook endpoints and event subscriptions (delivered, opened, clicked, failed, etc.)
API keys, domain-scoped keys, and any IP allowlists
Dedicated sending IPs and their warm-up state, if on a plan that includes them
Logs / event history for compliance — export before retention window expires
Tags and custom variables used for segmentation in logs
export_method: Use Mailgun's REST API with a master key: paginate /v3/<domain>/bounces, /unsubscribes, /complaints to JSON; pull /v3/<domain>/templates for templates; /v3/<domain>/routes for inbound rules; /v3/domains/<domain>/webhooks for webhook config; /v3/<domain>/events for historical logs. The dashboard also offers CSV export for suppressions on most plans. Save DNS records (DKIM/SPF/DMARC) separately — they are visible per domain in the Mailgun control panel.
import_method: In Resend: add each sending domain and publish the new DKIM/SPF records from Resend (you cannot reuse Mailgun's DKIM keys). Re-create API keys, then upload suppressions via the Resend API or dashboard (check current docs for the exact endpoint). Re-author templates as React Email components or plain HTML. Re-register webhooks pointing at the same internal handlers — verify event-name mapping. Switch SDK calls from `mailgun.js` to `resend` (or swap SMTP host/port/credentials if using SMTP). Run in shadow mode (dual-send or percentage cutover) before flipping DNS-level MX/inbound traffic.

concept_mapping.notes: Resend keys are scoped; recreate per environment
Both support SMTP relay; host and port differ — check Resend docs
Payload shape differs but concepts align

concept_mapping.mailgun: Webhooks (delivered, opened, clicked, failed, complained, unsubscribed)
concept_mapping.notes: Event names and signature scheme differ
Used for log filtering; rebuild any analytics that depended on them
Naming and capabilities differ; verify before relying on them
May need a third-party inbound provider or self-hosted relay
Use a dedicated validator (ZeroBounce, Kickbox, etc.)
Confirm current Resend region options before migrating EU data

effort: Small project: ~1–2 days for a single domain with a handful of templates and webhooks. Medium project: ~1–2 weeks if you have multiple domains, dedicated IPs to warm up, complex Routes, or large suppression lists.
gotchas: Mailgun inbound Routes have no direct Resend equivalent — if you rely on inbound parsing, plan a separate solution before cutover
Dedicated IP availability and pricing differ between providers; confirm current Resend plan terms and budget for warm-up time
EU vs US region: data residency matters for GDPR — make sure the new Resend domain is provisioned in the right region
Log / event retention windows differ — export Mailgun events you may need for audit before retiring the account
Suppression lists must be migrated before first send to avoid re-mailing users who bounced or unsubscribed
DKIM keys cannot be carried over; you need to publish Resend's DNS records and wait for propagation
Reputation does not transfer with the domain — if you switch sending IPs, expect a warm-up period
Mailgun's email validation and mailing-list features may require third-party replacements; budget for that

email.subject: Migrating our transactional email from Mailgun to Resend
email.body:
Hi team,

We're moving our transactional email from Mailgun to Resend. Both are REST + SMTP providers, so application code changes are limited, but a few items need coordination:

1. DNS — we'll publish new DKIM/SPF records from Resend per sending domain. Mailgun's keys cannot be reused.
2. Suppressions — bounces, unsubscribes, and complaints will be exported from Mailgun and imported into Resend before first production send.
3. Templates — existing Handlebars templates will be re-authored (React Email or plain HTML).
4. Webhooks — endpoints stay the same, but event names and signatures will be updated.
5. Inbound Routes / email validation — no direct Resend equivalent; we'll evaluate a third-party option.

We'll cut over in shadow mode first, then flip traffic. Expect questions about deliverability during the switch — please flag any anomalies.

Thanks.
